title: Fitting a plywood sub-floor over timber joists
subtitle: Lay 18 mm exterior-grade plywood over joists to create a flat, rigid base
excerpt: How to lay 18 mm exterior-grade plywood sheets over timber joists to create a flat, stable sub-floor suitable for tiling, LVT, or engineered wood, covering joist layout, sheet staggering, and screw fixing at correct spacing.
sourceNotes: BS 8203: code of practice for installation of sheet floor coverings. TRADA Wood Information Sheets. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A structural sheet material fixed directly to joists to create the base for a finished floor covering. For sub-floor use, exterior-grade plywood (WBP bond, or C/D grade) is specified because it tolerates occasional moisture. 18 mm thickness is the standard for joists at 400 to 600 mm centres.

Laying sheet material so that the end joints of one row do not align with the end joints of adjacent rows. A half-sheet stagger (offset by 1200 mm on a 2400 mm sheet) gives structural continuity across the floor and prevents a running joint that allows the floor to move as a unit.

content.text: A
sub-floor plywood

layer is fitted over timber joists when the finish floor requires a rigid, flat base that the joists alone cannot provide. Tiling requires a minimum of 18 mm plywood over the joists; engineered wood glued-down also needs a rigid base. The same method is used when replacing a badly-damaged old board floor: strip the boards, inspect the joists, and sheet over them in plywood before laying the new finish.
What you will need: materials

18 mm exterior-grade plywood (C/D grade, WBP bonded) in 2440 x 1220 mm sheets. No. 8 x 50 mm countersunk woodscrews (ring-shank screws hold better than plain-shank in a floor). The number of sheets: calculate the floor area plus 15 per cent for cuts and waste. Allow the sheets to acclimatise in the room for 48 hours before laying.
Step 1, inspect the joists

Before laying any sheet, inspect each joist for rot, woodworm, or sagging. Press a screwdriver blade firmly into the top edge of each joist: it should not penetrate. Replace any joist that the blade sinks into without resistance. Check that all joists are at the same height with the long spirit level laid across them: a joist that is high causes a bump in the finished floor; a joist that is low creates a hollow. Pack low joists with timber shims and plane or sand high joists back to the common level.
Step 2, mark the joist positions on the sheets

Mark the joist centres on the wall at each side of the room before the sheets go down. These marks let you find the joists for screwing once the sheets cover them. Use a chalk line to snap a line across the top of the sheets marking each joist position after the first sheet is laid. The screws must land in the joist, not in the gap between: a missed screw adds nothing and weakens the sheet.
Step 3, lay the first sheet row

Position the first sheet with its long edge across the joists (not parallel to them). Leave a 2 mm gap at the wall and between adjacent sheets: this expansion gap prevents buckling as the plywood moves with humidity. Do not force sheets tight together. Cut sheets to length with the circular saw or jigsaw, keeping the good face up to minimise tear-out. The end of each sheet must land on a joist: if the joist spacing means an end falls short, add a nogging (a short timber block) between joists at that point to give the sheet end a bearing.
Step 4, screw at correct spacing

Drive No. 8 x 50 mm screws at 150 mm centres along each joist line across the sheet. Screws at the sheet edges go at 100 mm centres. Pre-drill and countersink every hole: a screw that drives without pre-drilling can split the ply veneer and pull through without gripping. Drive each screw until the head is just flush with or slightly below the plywood surface. A proud screw head will telegraph through the finished floor covering.
Step 5, stagger the end joints
The end joints of the second row must be
staggered

by at least 600 mm from the end joints of the first row. Start the second row with a half sheet to achieve this. The long-edge joints between rows can align across the floor; the end joints must not. After all sheets are fixed, walk the floor and check for any movement or bounce. Any movement means a screw has missed the joist or a joist is sitting too low.
Variations

For a bathroom floor that will receive tiles, use 18 mm WBP plywood and seal all cut edges with a waterproof PVA primer before tiling. The plywood is structural only; the tile adhesive provides the water resistance. Do not use chipboard (particleboard) under tiles: chipboard swells when wet and cracks the tile bond.
Care

A correctly fixed plywood sub-floor needs no maintenance and outlasts the finish floor on top of it. If a section feels springy, check whether the screws in that area have pulled through the sheet: add additional screws at 100 mm centres in the affected zone. If a whole bay feels soft, inspect the joist below for moisture damage.

attrs.symptom: Sheet edges ridge up slightly at the joints
attrs.cause: No expansion gap left between sheets; or sheets were not acclimatised and have expanded after laying.
attrs.fix: If the ridge is less than 1 mm it can be sanded flush with the sanding block. If the ridge is greater than 1 mm, the sheets may need the edge cut back 2 mm with a router or plane to restore the gap.

attrs.symptom: Floor bounces or flexes in one area after all sheets are fixed
attrs.cause: Screws missed the joist; joist is sitting low; or the joist end-bearing has failed.
attrs.fix: Snap a chalk line across the sheet to locate the joist accurately and add screws along that line. If the bounce persists, inspect the joist from below or from a neighbouring room: a joist that has dropped off its bearing needs structural repair.

attrs.symptom: Sheet splits along the grain as a screw is driven
attrs.cause: Screw driven too close to the sheet edge; no pilot hole.
attrs.fix: Withdraw the screw and drill a full pilot hole. Keep screws at least 12 mm from the sheet edge. A strip of masking tape over the surface at the screw position can also prevent the top veneer from tearing.
